title: The Imparfait
description: A2 introduction to the French imperfect tense: formation from the nous-present stem, the full set of endings, and the three key uses -- description/background, habitual past ('used to'), and ongoing past ('was doing'). Contrasted with the passé composé from A1. Explained in English.

cards.back: the imperfect tense -- used for description, habits, and ongoing past
cards.notes: In A1 the passé composé was your only past tense. Now the imparfait covers a different job: **description and background** (*il faisait beau* -- the weather was nice), **habits** (*je jouais* -- I used to play), and **ongoing actions** (*elle dormait* -- she was sleeping).

cards.back: drop -ons from the nous present to get the imparfait stem
cards.notes: The rule: take the **nous** form of the present tense and remove **-ons**. Examples: *nous parlons → parl-*, *nous finissons → finiss-*, *nous mangeons → mange-*, *nous buvons → buv-*. The only exception is **être**, whose stem is **ét-**.

cards.back: the six imparfait endings (same for all verbs)
cards.notes:
These endings attach to the imparfait stem for every verb -- regular or irregular:

| Subject | Ending | Example (parler) |
|---|---|---|
| je | **-ais** | je parl**ais** |
| tu | **-ais** | tu parl**ais** |
| il/elle/on | **-ait** | il parl**ait** |
| nous | **-ions** | nous parl**ions** |
| vous | **-iez** | vous parl**iez** |
| ils/elles | **-aient** | ils parl**aient** |

cards.front: être → j'étais, tu étais, il était…
cards.back: être is the only irregular imparfait stem: ét-
cards.notes: **être** does not follow the nous-drop rule. Its imparfait stem is **ét-** (irregular). Full conjugation: *j'étais, tu étais, il/elle était, nous étions, vous étiez, ils/elles étaient*.
cards.hint: ét- is the only imparfait stem you must memorize separately.

cards.front: Il faisait beau. / Elle était fatiguée.
cards.notes: Use the imparfait to **paint a scene** -- describing weather, feelings, appearances, and states at a point in the past. English equivalents: *it was nice out*, *she was tired*. These are background conditions, not completed events.

cards.front: Quand j'étais petit, je jouais au foot.
cards.back: imparfait for habits and repeated past actions ('used to')
cards.notes: When something happened **repeatedly** or **habitually** in the past, use the imparfait. The English translation is often **'used to'** or a simple past that implies routine: *When I was young, I used to play football.* Contrast: passé composé (completed once) vs. imparfait (repeated/habitual).

cards.front: Elle lisait quand je suis arrivé.
cards.back: imparfait for ongoing past actions ('was/were doing')
cards.notes: For an action that was **in progress** when something else happened, use the imparfait. English: *She was reading when I arrived.* The passé composé (*je suis arrivé*) marks the interrupting completed event; the imparfait (*elle lisait*) marks the background ongoing action.
cards.hint: Imparfait = the movie playing in the background; passé composé = the plot event.

cards.front: J'habitais à Lyon. (imparfait) vs. J'ai habité à Lyon. (passé composé)
cards.back: imparfait = ongoing/habitual; passé composé = completed event
cards.notes:
Both are past tense, but:
- **Imparfait**: *J'habitais à Lyon* -- I used to live in Lyon / I was living in Lyon (state or habit, no defined end).
- **Passé composé**: *J'ai habité à Lyon* -- I lived in Lyon (completed, now over).

English often uses the same simple past for both, which is why choosing between them is the core A2 challenge.

cards.back: to speak/talk -- I was speaking / I used to speak
cards.notes: Stem: *nous parlons* → **parl-**. Add **-ais**: *je parl**ais***. All persons: *je parlais, tu parlais, il parlait, nous parlions, vous parliez, ils parlaient*.

cards.back: to finish -- I was finishing / I used to finish
cards.notes: Stem: *nous finissons* → **finiss-**. Add **-ais**: *je finiss**ais***. Note the double-s in the stem -- this is the -ir verb pattern visible here.

cards.back: to have -- I had / I used to have
cards.notes: Stem: *nous avons* → **av-**. Add **-ais**: *j'av**ais***. Full: *j'avais, tu avais, il avait, nous avions, vous aviez, ils avaient*. Very common in descriptions: *J'avais faim* -- I was hungry.

cards.back: to do/make -- I was doing / I used to do
cards.notes: Stem: *nous faisons* → **fais-**. Add **-ais**: *je fais**ais***. Common in weather: *Il faisait beau* -- the weather was nice / it was nice out.

cards.back: to go -- I was going / I used to go
cards.notes: Stem: *nous allons* → **all-**. Add **-ais**: *j'all**ais***. Useful for habits: *J'allais au marché chaque samedi* -- I used to go to the market every Saturday.

steps.title: Forming the imparfait: stem + endings
steps.body:
# The Imparfait -- Formation

In A1 the passé composé was your only past tense; it covers completed actions. The **imparfait** covers the rest: description, habit, and ongoing actions. Same past time frame, different role.

## Step 1 -- Find the stem

Take the **nous** form of the present tense and **drop -ons**:

| Infinitive | Nous present | Imparfait stem |
|---|---|---|
| parler | nous parl**ons** | **parl-** |
| finir | nous finiss**ons** | **finiss-** |
| faire | nous fais**ons** | **fais-** |
| boire | nous buv**ons** | **buv-** |
| avoir | nous av**ons** | **av-** |

> **Exception:** **être** is the only irregular stem -- it is **ét-** (*j'étais, tu étais*...).

## Step 2 -- Add the endings

These six endings are the same for **every** verb:

| Subject | Ending |
|---|---|
| je | **-ais** |
| tu | **-ais** |
| il / elle / on | **-ait** |
| nous | **-ions** |
| vous | **-iez** |
| ils / elles | **-aient** |

Full example -- *parler*: *je parlais, tu parlais, il parlait, nous parlions, vous parliez, ils parlaient.*

steps.title: When to use the imparfait
steps.body:
## The three uses of the imparfait

### 1. Description and background
Setting the scene -- weather, feelings, appearances, states:

- *Il **faisait** beau.* -- The weather was nice.
- *Elle **était** fatiguée.* -- She was tired.
- *La maison **semblait** vide.* -- The house seemed empty.

### 2. Habits and repeated past actions ('used to')
Something that happened **regularly** in the past:

- *Quand j'**étais** petit, je **jouais** au foot.* -- When I was young, I used to play football.
- *Chaque été, nous **allions** à la mer.* -- Every summer we used to go to the sea.

English signal words: *used to, would (habitual), every day, always, often, when I was young.*

### 3. Ongoing past action ('was/were doing')
An action **in progress** when something else happened:

- *Elle **lisait** quand je suis arrivé.* -- She was reading when I arrived.
- *Il **dormait** pendant que tu téléphonais.* -- He was sleeping while you were on the phone.

The interrupting completed event takes the **passé composé**; the background action takes the **imparfait**.

steps.title: Imparfait vs. passé composé
steps.body:
## Choosing between the two past tenses

Both refer to the past -- but they answer different questions:

| Question | Tense | Example |
|---|---|---|
| What was happening? (background) | **imparfait** | *Il neigeait.* (It was snowing.) |
| What happened? (completed event) | **passé composé** | *Je suis sorti.* (I went out.) |
| What did you use to do? (habit) | **imparfait** | *Je courais chaque matin.* (I used to run every morning.) |
| What did you do once? (single event) | **passé composé** | *J'ai couru 10 km hier.* (I ran 10 km yesterday.) |

## The English trap

English often uses the **same simple past** for both:

- *She lived in Paris.* → Could be **imparfait** (*Elle habitait à Paris* -- ongoing, no end stated) **or** passé composé (*Elle a habité à Paris* -- completed, now over).

Ask yourself: **habit / description / in-progress → imparfait; completed single event → passé composé.**

## Quick test

- Does 'used to' or 'was/were ...-ing' fit in English? → **imparfait**
- Could you say 'once' or 'suddenly'? → **passé composé**

steps.title: Match verb to its imparfait stem

exercise.prompt: Match each infinitive to its imparfait stem (remember: drop -ons from the nous present).
Translate: *The weather was nice.* (use faire -- il faisait...)
exercise.hint: faire: stem fais- + -ait (il) = faisait. Weather uses il as subject.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the correct imparfait form of *jouer* (je) -- I used to play.
exercise.sentence: Quand j'étais petit, je ___ au foot tous les jours.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *We used to live in Paris.* (habiter -- nous)
Arrange the words to say: *He was sleeping when I called.* (dormir: il dormait; appeler passé composé: j'ai appelé)
exercise.hint: Background ongoing action (dormait) + completed interrupting event (j'ai appelé).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *She was tired.* (être -- elle étais or était?)
exercise.hint: être: irregular stem ét- + -ait (il/elle) = était. Don't forget to agree the adjective: fatiguée (feminine).

steps.title: Which sentence uses the imparfait correctly?

exercise.prompt: A person describes their childhood routine. Which sentence is correct French?

images.label: Chaque matin, je mangeais des céréales.
Chaque matin, j'ai mangé des céréales.
Chaque matin, je mangeait des céréales.
Chaque matin, je mange des céréales.

exercise.hint: A repeated childhood routine calls for the imparfait. Also check: je (not il) takes -ais, not -ait.
distractors: Chaque matin, j'ai mangé des céréales.
Chaque matin, je mangeait des céréales.
Chaque matin, je mange des céréales.

steps.title: Fill in the imparfait -- ongoing action

exercise.prompt: Fill in the imparfait of *lire* (elle) -- she was reading. (Stem: nous lisons → lis-)
exercise.sentence: Elle ___ quand le téléphone a sonné.

steps.title: Match the English cue to the correct tense

exercise.prompt: Match each English phrase to the tense it signals in French.

pairs.left: I used to walk to school.

steps.title: Build: Every summer we went to the sea

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *Every summer, we used to go to the sea.* (aller -- nous allions)
Translate: *I was hungry.* (literally: I had hunger -- avoir faim; use je)
exercise.hint: avoir: stem av- + -ais (je) = avais. 'To be hungry' in French is avoir faim (to have hunger).
